gate_type: no gate — open handler (public GET parameters only; no nonce, no capability check, no secret)
reasoning: The code path to the SQL sink is gated only by is_search(), which checks for publicly controllable GET parameters (?dgwt_wcas=1&post_type=product&s=query) — any unauthenticated user can satisfy this check trivially. There is no nonce, no authentication cookie, and no capability check protecting the path. However, the SQL injection itself is NOT directly exploitable by an unauthenticated attacker: the filter 'dgwt/wcas/search_page/result_post_ids' is populated by Search::getProductIds() (Search.php lines 949-954), which returns $this->postsIDsBuffer built from $post->ID values sourced from get_posts() — these are always integers from the database. An unauthenticated attacker cannot directly inject non-integer values into the filter output in a default WordPress install; doing so requires a second co-installed plugin, mu-plugin, or theme that hooks 'dgwt/wcas/search_page/result_post_ids' and returns attacker-controlled non-integer elements. No such hook exists in this plugin itself. Additionally, the vulnerable function only fires when the WOOF Products Filter plugin is also installed and active (it hooks 'woof_get_filtered_price_query', a WOOF-specific filter). The exploitation chain requires: (1) WOOF plugin active, (2) ajax-search-for-woocommerce active, (3) a third co-installed component hooking the public filter with non-integer values. Without a cooperating second component, an unauthenticated attacker has no direct path to inject into the SQL. The flaw represents a missing defense-in-depth control (no intval cast / no $wpdb->prepare()) at a public WordPress filter trust boundary, not a directly exploitable vulnerability in default installs. Severity is downgraded to LOW.
